pain_point_label: Misalignment with Student Academic Load & Mental Health

matches.match_rationale: Student records contain structured data — grades, number of clubs enrolled, attendance times, sleep survey responses — that can be fed into a classifier to flag students at high burnout or exclusion risk. This directly addresses the absence of holistic wellness metrics by operationalizing the structured signals schools already collect. For a 500+ staff operator, the feature vectors are readily available from existing SIS and activity sign-up systems.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A burnout-risk classifier ingests each student's academic load score, number of co-curricular commitments, late submission frequency, and attendance patterns to produce a daily risk tier (low/medium/high) surfaced to counselors and homeroom teachers.

matches.match_rationale: Propensity modeling can predict which individual students are most likely to experience burnout, academic decline, or mental health deterioration based on their current co-curricular load, historical grade trajectories, and demographic features. This directly targets the causal mechanism described — no structured well-being metric — by building a leading indicator before a student reaches crisis point. Education operators with multi-year historical data on student activity loads and outcomes have exactly the longitudinal data this approach requires.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A student wellness propensity model scores every student weekly on likelihood of academic decline or welfare intervention need, enabling pastoral teams to proactively contact high-risk students rather than responding reactively.

matches.match_rationale: Co-curricular enrolment is fundamentally a constraint satisfaction problem: each student's combination of clubs, teams, and academic subjects must satisfy hard constraints (load caps, academic standing thresholds) and soft constraints (student preference, program balance, equity across achievement tiers). This directly addresses the missing co-curricular load caps the pain point identifies as root cause. No adequate tool currently exists to enforce these constraints at enrolment time across a multi-site operator.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An AI-assisted co-curricular enrolment system automatically checks whether a student's proposed activity combination violates load caps or academic thresholds, suggests alternative combinations, and flags low-performing students for inclusion in lower-intensity offerings rather than outright exclusion.

matches.match_rationale: A multi-turn conversational agent can serve as a private, low-stigma wellness check-in interface for students — collecting self-reported stress, sleep, and workload data through natural dialogue that a paper survey or counselor visit cannot replicate at scale. This directly addresses the absence of structured rest and well-being assessment by creating a continuous, scalable data collection channel. Education operators in this segment are already trialling digital pastoral tools, making conversational AI a credible adoption path.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A daily two-minute chatbot check-in (web or WhatsApp) asks students about sleep, stress level, and after-school schedule, feeding aggregated anonymous data to wellness dashboards and triggering counselor alerts when individual scores fall below threshold.

matches.match_rationale: Once risk data is available, text generation can automatically draft personalised well-being reports for parents and pastoral summaries for teachers — eliminating the manual reporting burden that currently prevents counselors from communicating proactively about student load concerns. This addresses the operational gap where even when staff identify at-risk students, communication is delayed because writing individualised messages at scale is infeasible.
matches.opportunity_sketch: Automated weekly wellness digest emails to parents of flagged students, generated from structured risk data and populated with specific observations about their child's load and recommended actions.
